Author: Brian Brown
Title: From Prompt to Prototype: My Return to the World of Code
Text:
From Prompt to Prototype: My Return to the World of Code
How a simple pickleball question led me down the AI rabbit hole.
The Nerd Swipe: A Quest for the Perfect Paddle
I just had one simple question, “What pickleball paddle should I buy?” How hard could that be to answer? It turns out to be much more complicated than I thought.
OK. It has been 9 months since I left Google and it is time for me to get out from under the rock I have been living under and see what the hell has been going on with the AI coding revolution. I’m kind of surprised it has taken me this long, but trying to answer my pickleball question I got nerd swiped into writing a data visualizer for pickleball data, and that led me down this rabbit hole. Before I begin, if you are interested in pickleball you should check out Johnkew Pickleball on YouTube. He has fantastic pickleball paddle reviews all backed with real data from his basement pickleball paddle testing lab.
As you might imagine, I have many friends who are still actively developing software either at Google or newly free from the Google toolchains, so it’s not like I am completely unaware, but until you experience these things firsthand—it just isn’t quite the same. It turns out a lot of things have changed in the past year and continue to rapidly develop, but from what I have read and heard from friends, we have passed a tipping point where we have moved from the majority of developers not having embraced using AI assistive tools to the majority of developers using AI tools day to day. With that in mind I wanted to document my journey through taking a simple project from vibe coding in Gemini Canvas to a more professional AI driven development workflow modeled after some newly evolving industry standard practices I have learned from friends and colleagues.
At first I thought I would document the journey and then write a post about the full journey, but that would probably be way too much stuff to pack into one post. So instead, I am going to post as I go along and maybe your feedback will shape the direction I take on this path.
The Mini Primer: How the AI Overlords Build Apps
For my non-technical friends, here is a mini primer on AI code generation. AI code generation tools use Large Language Models (LLMs) to generate code based on an English language (actually any natural language) prompt. LLMs have been trained by “reading” all of the code in the world to find patterns and predict the next word (or code) to output based on your prompt input. There are of course much more sophisticated explanations of how it all works, but in the end the model has learned a bunch of stuff by reading things on the internet, you provide it some context through a prompt and uploaded files, and it outputs a response in this case code you can run.
Five-Minute Magic: The “Wow” Factor of Gemini Canvas
Let’s start simple.
Gemini Canvas.
Go to the site https://gemini.google.com/
Click on Canvas under Tools.
Prompt Gemini to make an application that does whatever you want.
I think step 3 might actually be where many people get stuck. What do I actually want? This is where the real work is done. I just read an interesting Vibe Coding post from Cassie Kozyrkov who was a statistician for a long time at Google. Her post dives deeper into “How to Know What You Want.” This is where a lot of the real work is done, diving deep into ideas and coming up with compelling solutions. It turns out there is no magic bullet here, but it is where you can let your creativity and imagination take over.
Great, so then you did 1, 2, 3 and you’ve got yourself an app that does the thing you asked your AI overlords to build for you.
My initial reaction to this was, WOW!!! Gemini had just created an interactive web application in Javascript using the React framework and it looked pretty good for the first go. There were some minor UI bugs like overlapping labels, but for the most part things just worked. The CSV upload mechanism and parser worked, the selection UI updated all the data fields, the custom SVG renderer of the paddle updated based on different paddle characteristics. I was impressed, and most of all, I spent about five minutes crafting the initial prompt describing what I wanted. The model probably took about 5 minutes to churn on the code and output this result.
My initial prompt looked something like this:
Create an application that can visualize pickleball paddle data from a CSV file. Allow the user to select from the different paddles by manufacturer and name. Allow the sure to upload a CSV file with all of the data. Show the six key characteristics of the paddle Power, Pop, Spin, Swing Weight, Twist Weight, and Balance Point. Show a representation of the physical shape of the paddle including length, width, weight, thickness, generation, and paddle size. Show the paddle price and some indicator of how expensive the paddle is relative to others. Use a modern dark theme for the UI styling.
(I uploaded a sample CSV file with all of the column names set and some sample data with the initial prompt.)
I didn’t really know it at the time, but this is when I started down the vibe coding rabbit hole.
I had an initial prototype in minutes. Enough to hook me in to wanting to go a bit deeper. It was fun. It was exciting. It was 9:30 pm and I was about to stay up late (for me).
Lost in Translation: The Language of Bugs
So I had some bugs I wanted to fix. Overlapping labels in the UI. I tried things like, “Fix the label for the paddle height so it doesn’t overlap with the dimension line” That worked. Later I would just say things like, “Fix all of the labels in paddle UI so they maximize visibility and minimize overlap.” That would mostly work as well though sometimes you would get some weird results. Sometimes I wouldn’t really know what words to use to describe the thing I wanted fixed so I would flip over to the code tab, find the UI element I wanted fixed and use the name the LLM had given it. This worked, but felt like it was breaking my flow. Thus began my learning process of describing what I wanted with enough specificity, but balancing that with making more general statements so I didn’t have to prompt every tiny incremental change.
I have learned from my “pro” friends that you get past this by feeding guidelines to your LLM either by writing a UI style guideline in this case or referencing one that is already written. This is a pain to do in Gemini Canvas, because if you add this to your prompt at the beginning it works for a while, but then forgets. Alternatively you can prompt it every time, but this is tedious.
Gemini Canvas Limitation - Passing relevant context to your prompt in a consistent and easy way.
The Churn: One Step Forward, Two Steps Back
Sometimes when prompting to fix a bug or add a feature, things just break. You then have to prompt to fix the thing that broke. I found that sometimes a fix I had prompted to fix a couple of turns (back and forth with the LLM) ago just disappears in the latest version. This can be super frustrating because you often don’t notice until the change has gone by for a while then you are not quite sure what or when it broke. In traditional software development this problem is mitigated by using a version control system for your code and by having a testing framework to make sure things don’t break unexpectedly.
You can kind of mitigate this in Gemini Canvas by clicking the “share” link which generates a snapshot of the code that is shareable with others. Unfortunately this loses all of the back and forth with the model and just copies the code. I did this as a pseudo version control system, emailing myself a link (I could have used a doc) with a change description like, “Ridge regularization for inverse stability”.
To get around the lack of testing framework, it is possible to have tests embedded into the application that get run and throw errors during compilation. If they throw errors during compilation, they can be recognized by the model and fixed without prompting, otherwise you have to run the tests from the UI in the application and manually feed back (copy and paste) errors to a prompt to get fixed. This works but is pretty tedious.
Gemini Canvas Limitation - No real version control system for the code or testing framework.
The Token Crawl: When Magic Gets Slow
When you start out with your initial prompt everything seems great. You put in a short prompt and the LLM outputs a few hundred lines of code. It takes a few minutes, but you just went from nothing to something and it feels like magic. Then you just want to make a tiny fix. Change the color from blue to red. Move this button. Change this text. It seems small, but depending on what’s happening in the model things can get really slow and as your application gets bigger and bigger things get slower and slower. This is because for the most part the model is outputting the entire application every time you go back and forth with a prompt. In my experience with this project, sometimes there is some optimization and large chunks of the app that are unchanged are output very quickly, but other times things stream out line by line, token by token and it can get really slow, 5, 10, 15 minutes slow for seemingly small changes.
There isn’t much of a mitigation for this in Gemini Canvas other than to keep your application small and focused. You can prompt the model to simplify the code or modularize the code which may help a bit, but as your application gets bigger things just keep getting slower.
Gemini Canvas Limitation - Code generation recreates the entire app most of the time making things slow.
There are of course similar offerings from OpenAI, Anthropic, and Microsoft in this space and surely many others. Anthropic was first in this space with Claude Artifacts in June 2024 with wide availability in August 2024. OpenAI followed with ChatGPT Canvas in October 2024. Microsoft has Github CoPilot Workspace which is a bit more advanced and integrated with a full-fledged development workflow introduced in April 2024, but isn’t quite the same and uses OpenAI and Anthropic models under the hood anyway.
I’m not sure if these other offerings have similar limitations or not, but based on the way they are structured these are likely to be challenges for at least Claude Artifacts and ChatGPT Canvas.
Success Without Mastery: The Prototype Payoff
In the end I was able to create a pretty cool pickleball paddle data visualizer that runs on desktop and mobile. It calculates a similarity score to find similar-performing paddles potentially for a much cheaper price. There is a really cool 3D point cloud visualizer that shows similar paddle clusters which makes exploring the data super fun for real data nerds. And maybe most importantly I learned a ton about how to develop prototype applications with Gemini Canvas and some of its limitations.
Time from first prompt to my latest version spanned 4 days and I probably put in 8-10 hours of actual work. Was it faster than a traditional coding session? Definitely. Do I now know how to program in React? Absolutely not. Will this thing stand the test of time? Nope. Did it serve its purpose of exploring a bunch of ideas quickly? Yes.
Breaking Out of the Sandbox
So, what’s next?
Well I think it is time to move on from Gemini Canvas and move to a more professional AI assisted development workflow. There are a bunch of open questions:
Should I use a fully integrated AI coding IDE like Cursor, Windsurf, Github Copilot, Firebase Studio or Antigravity?
Should I go to a full agent based workflow like Claude Code, Codex CLI, or Gemini CLI?
Should I go fully cloud native in the browser and keep using my 5-year-old Pixelbook or get a brand new Macbook Pro M5 Max in a couple weeks and run all my compilation locally for maximum efficiency and power?
There are so many new development workflows possible with an infinite combination of tools, but based on some feedback from friends I will likely move slowly toward something that looks a bit more like the professional workflow described here, but I would love to hear from anyone that has an opinion on this. What have you experienced and what direction would you go?
Brian Brown
PS. When I shared this story with a friend they asked, “but have you chosen a pickleball paddle?” Well the answer to that is no, not yet. I will keep you all posted. Pickleball has been one of the fun things I have started doing more of with my wife after we retired. It has been the source of some really fun times and now a reason to start coding again.
